title: Good place to work for as a contractor
body: Good place to work for as a contractor
pros: I worked for them as an independent contractor for a bit over 1 year, The pay was quite good. It was a fully remote job that ended abruptly because the client wanted a PM that actually knew about the tech we were using, but for some reason Jacobs wouldn't provide that, so when it came up for renewal they lost out to a smaller, specialized company. This was unfortunate because I really enjoyed this job and got along very well with the client. It was also fully remote which was great. Overall, at least as an independent contractor, working at Jacobs was great
cons: The only downsides I could say about my time here is that they need to listen to the client more and try better to give them what they need. Also, they often paid out our earnings quite late.

title: Healthy Work Life Balance
pros: My experience with Jacobs has been positive in terms of a welcoming company culture (specifically at the local level) and a supportive work-life balance. The people that I work with are kind and inviting to new members despite an obvious age gap between entry/intermediate staff and senior staff. Jacobs has an unlimited PTO policy that in my experience has been generous. I have not had any time off requests denied and have been able to take a healthy amount of paid vacation per year since the unlimited PTO policy was put in place.
cons: The company, like many corporations, is very secretive about the pay scales. The annual merit increases are small, regardless of an individual's performance rating. And the actual salary range for your position is not disclosed.

pros: Great pay, a lot of diverse projects in many industries and opportunities to move around and learn, lot of highly knowledgeable and skilled coworkers, very diverse culture and international footprint, your experience could vary depending on your chain of command.
cons: âUnlimited PTOâ is a scam to keep you on 12-month rolling PTO (donât go over 4 weeks!) no accrual, no payout if you quit. If you are light on client work and bill to overhead, as directed âpubliclyâ, expect to get a talking to behind closed doors. Expect to say yes to too many things to maximize billable hours then regret your decision when youâre overworked and get a raise thatâs just a tad less than inflation for your exceeding performance.

title: Jacobs is a great place to work
body: Jacobs is a great place to work
pros: For a large company, Jacobs is an outstanding place to work. If you like working on interesting and exciting projects, and working with a company who actually values employee health, this is the place for you. From management, to colleagues everyone has a hard working, fair, and non-judgmental demeanor about the work you do. You are invited to set goals within the company but there isn't too much if any focus on micromanaging your work. My opinions have felt welcomed at all levels of project conversations, and my team has been very collaborative.
cons: As with any large company, sometimes you feel a bit lost in the system. That being said, Jacobs provides various networks you can be a part of to increase visibility to other projects or teams should you need to change projects. Projects can also change or run out of funding without much notice causing some uneasy times while you wait for new work to arrive. You have to be proactive within and market your own skills.

title: Jacobs has really screwed up in trying to get into the defense and intelligence space.
body: Jacobs has really screwed up in trying to get into the defense and intelligence space.
pros: This review is specifically for their work in the defense and intelligence space whereas the bulk of their work is in the engineering and infrastructure space. They have great benefits: High 401k matching and 5 weeks PTO. After being laid off, numerous program managers from other contracts worked diligently to help me find a job within Jacobs. Unfortunately it did not work out due to forces outside my control as well as those managers but their effort was there.
cons: Low pay for the defense and intelligence space in the Washington, DC area (very high cost of living). Terrible on-site program management; management personnel who were retained after Jacobs acquired The Buffalo Group. Those managers were woefully under-qualified for their new positions with Jacobs and did not exhibit any leadership qualities. Three different Jacobs on-site managers failed to back me up when the customer wronged me. Two of those same on-site managers blatantly lied to me, stabbed me in the back, and I ultimately lost my job after being brushed-in with a group of employees getting laid off due to a contract reduction. There were plenty of billets available for good analysts and I could have had one of them if Jacobs on-site managers would have simply better better humans. I should have reported this all to HR but was afraid of reprisal at the time.

title: An engineering juggernaut now in the consulting world..!
body: An engineering juggernaut now in the consulting world..!
cons: In large organizations like Jacobs, challenges grow in parallel with the company's expansion. As the organization scales, these challenges become more critical and visible. Jacobs, like many companies, often focuses on reducing overhead costs to increase profitability. This approach can sometimes lead to employees feeling like just another 40-hour billable figure, easily replaceable by someone else in the market. This perception of job insecurity can be demoralizing, especially for those who have dedicated many years to the company. There are individuals who have been with Jacobs for over 35 years, and while they are experts in their fields, they may not always be tech-savvy. It's crucial for Jacobs' leadership to place these experienced employees in roles that leverage their strengths, rather than in random positions where they might struggle and inadvertently cause issues due to a lack of specific skills.

title: Avoid this company at all costs
body: Avoid this company at all costs
pros: Pay is good. But that is the only thing that is remotely good.
cons: I joined the new Power and Energy Dept. on July 19, 2023 to work remote. I received a "PIP" Performance Improvement Plan In April 2024. They explained my "billability" was too low. I was sitting at home for weeks at a time begging my supervisor that I no work assignments and my "billability" slipped below 50%. Any assignment I did receive involved extensive multiple travel to the jobsite or the project never materialized. So why am I to blame for not having enough work? The corporate guerillas set me up for failure. Fortunate for me I knew they were going to boot me out so I already had a job lined up before my firing date. 14 engineers have left this company in the last couople of months. Avoid this company. They had no intention wanting me to proceed. IF you do not comply with the corporate guerillas, then you will be cast out.
role: Former employee - Senior Power System Engineer

title: Excellent company
pros: Jacobs has had some growing pains over the years, but ultimately I think that they are company that actually tries to give their employees a good work experience. From our flexible and hybrid work schedule to the personalized PTO they do try to encourage work life balance. The benefits are really good and they expand to free therapy sessions and other specialized benefits. I also feel that there is not a culture of pushing yourself beyond your limits because of a dedication to good work. Consulting is a demanding job, but Jacobs does well at remembering their biggest asset is the people.
cons: Really big company, so you donât know your coworkers and âoffice cultureâ is tough to build. There is a large emphasis on global work which is difficult to manage and still hasnât been figured out. You can feel distant from upper management because it is so large.

title: Only if you love NASA and no one else is hiring you
body: Only if you love NASA and no one else is hiring you
pros: The flexibility and flex Friday are great. I can always come earlier to work and move my week hours however I want. For example, if I want to work 12 hours one day and leave earlier on Thursday or Friday that's an option. Management does not care as long as I complete tasks on time.
cons: There are no diverse options for employee development. If you want to aim higher, you can only evolve into project-management positions. Since the JETS II contract is a government service, do not expect good salaries. If you want to earn good money, your only option is to become a senior project manager. The benefits are fine. They can solely hire you for a specific project, and then the next year NASA won't approve a budget for the project so they will likely move you to a project that you won't enjoy. Projects are always being impacted by NASA budgetary problems, so they will move you around a lot.

title: Great company for anyone with a historic preservation background
body: Great company for anyone with a historic preservation background
pros: Great company for anyone in the historic preservation field to work at, be fairly compensated, and get involved in large, interesting projects. Coworkers are nice, accommodating, communicative, and overall normal and nice human beings. I came from a toxic consulting work environment and was afraid Jacobs would be more or less the same -- that has not been my experience (although it is a big company so I do not know other's experience). In the CRM field, it is nice to be actually fairly compensated. I was offered $30K MORE at Jacobs compared to a small CRM firm within the same month. Lastly, they truly value employee referrals indicating that they believe their people are great and can find good people as well. They are quick to support you in your career growth and attendance at conferences. The benefits are great. Unlimited PTO, 401k matching, HSA account with employer contributions? Yes, please.
cons: Fully remote positions can always be hard when you never see/meet colleagues in person, but at Jacobs that depends on the group you are in. Most make an effort for team bonding and are always up for a break and a chat.

title: Repitative work and Management dosent care
body: Repitative work and Management dosent care
pros: Work life balance is good. Unlimited PTO
cons: Management is very poor. They dont care if you are really making any progress in your career. They will just assign you tasks and let you figure out everything on your own without proper support and guidance. Only 3% raise per year and some years they wont even do that unless you take the initiative to ask about it. Also felt largely undervalued as repitative tasks were given and no initiative was taken to know the likes and dislikes. No proper training given. Very less opportunity for growth and feels biased. Construction industry is male dominated largely and being a woman I felt that, its no different here. Also you wont get any bonuses. You will start feeling the burnout due lack of good management and managers who take responsibilty and are considerate about the employees under them

title: Fired with no notice
pros: My coworkers were all great people that worked hard, that's the only pro.
cons: Received an email for a meeting at 830 for a meeting at 930. Was fired on the spot alongside 50 other people "due to budgets for the new option year." No severance, no warning, threatened to be escorted from the building when we asked questions. But on the bright side they offered us a box for our belongings, so that was super awesome of them... Our team lead didn't even know this was happening. An executive stood in the back of the room silently looming over us all during the meeting. Incredibly shady company that will fire you for absolutely no reason. The turnover is on average one year at Jacobs. Avoid at all costs.

title: Great corporate vision somewhat embraced at business unit level
body: Great corporate vision somewhat embraced at business unit level
pros: Corporate level provides progressive roadmaps and tools for employee development. The programs are rolled out for business units to implement. On the surface it appears to be employee oriented and customer oriented. Very large corporation with potential several business units world wide.
cons: A new CEO recently took over and announced the spinoff of the technology group. Uncertainty about the future; rumored we will be sold off. Minimal evidence of our business unit making efforts to embrace the corporate vision for DEI. Specifically minority representation in positions of leadership are minimal. Diversity includes more than just white women at the Sr. & Exec management levels. To date there are no people of color in positions above line management after 10-years on a government contract.

title: Whenever I think of a bad employerâ¦
body: Whenever I think of a bad employerâ¦
pros: Stable contracts A variety of job options
cons: This more aimed at the former Buffalo Group than Jacobs. I was on a stable contract with terrible leadership. I would constantly be contacted outside of working hours, I was called in last minute with no warning causing me to cut my family trip short, and once I requested PTO and when I didnât show up I was blamed by my manager for him not doing his job! I was also treated poorly for standing up for myself on these kinds of things. I work for a better company now and it is a stark difference. I get treated respectfully like an actual person with rights. Iâm aware that some of the things Iâve said here are standard in some work environments but if you are like me you want betterâ¦and I encourage you to be very careful about taking a job here. The pay is also extremely low for the industry and location in NOVA.

cons: Some roles are focused on one type of project only. That could stifle creativity if varying project types is important to you.

title: Great culture for a large publicly traded company
body: Great culture for a large publicly traded company
pros: Great culture. A lot of employee resources and communities of practices. A lot of experts in almost any part of the engineering consulting industry. There are some nice diversity inclusion groups here: -ACE (disability, health challenges, neurodiversity) remove barriers that create exclusion; -Careers Network; -ENLACE (LatinX); -Harambee (Black recruitment, retention, empowerment, and advancement); -OneWorld (cross-cultural / global connections); -PRISM (LGBTQ+); -VetNet (for veterans); -Women's Network (gender equality / challenge the norms)
cons: It is a large firm. There are specific thresholds for winning work and pursuing contracts. If they are small one-off projects or small revenue work then likely won't be considered.
